Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## ONLINE HYPERPARAMETER META-LEARNING WITH HYPERGRADIENT DISTILLATION

**Hae Beom Lee**[1] **, Hayeon Lee**[1] **, Jaewoong Shin**[3] **, Eunho Yang**[1] _[,]_[2] **, Timothy M. Hospedales**[4] _[,]_[5] **, Sung Ju Hwang**[1] _[,]_[2] KAIST[1] , AITRICS[2] , Lunit[3] , South Korea, University of Edinburgh[4] , Samsung AI Centre, Cambridge[5] , United Kingdom _{_ haebeom.lee, hayeon926, shinjw148, eunhoy, sjhwang82 _}_ @kaist.ac.kr _{_ t.hospedales _}_ @ed.ac.uk

## ABSTRACT

Many gradient-based meta-learning methods assume a set of parameters that do not participate in inner-optimization, which can be considered as _hyperparameters_ . Although such hyperparameters can be optimized using the existing gradientbased hyperparameter optimization (HO) methods, they suffer from the following issues. Unrolled differentiation methods do not scale well to high-dimensional hyperparameters or horizon length , Implicit Function Theorem (IFT) based methods are restrictive for online optimization, and short horizon approximations suffer from short horizon bias. In this work, we propose a novel HO method that can overcome these limitations, by approximating the second-order term with knowledge distillation. Specifically, we parameterize a single Jacobian-vector product (JVP) for each HO step and minimize the distance from the true second-order term. Our method allows online optimization and also is scalable to the hyperparameter dimension and the horizon length. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our method on two different meta-learning methods and three benchmark datasets.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Meta-learning (Schmidhuber, 1987; Thrun & Pratt, 1998) aims to learn a learning process itself over a task distribution. Many gradient-based meta-learning approaches assume a set of parameters that do not participate in inner-optimization (Lee & Choi, 2018; Flennerhag et al., 2019; Raghu et al., 2019) which can be seen as _hyperparameters_ . Those hyperparameters are important in helping the inner-learner converge faster and generalize better. As they are usually very high-dimensional such as element-wise learning rates (Li et al., 2017), we cannot meta-learn them with simple hyperparameter optimization (HO) techniques such as random search (Bergstra & Bengio, 2012) or Bayesian optimization (Snoek et al., 2012) due to the too extensive search space.

In this case, we can use gradient-based HO methods that can directly optimize the highdimensional hyperparameters by minimizing the validation loss w.r.t. the hyperparameters (Bengio, 2000). Due to the expensive computational cost of evaluating the hypergradients (i.e. the gradient w.r.t. the hyperparameters), there has been a lot of efforts to improve the effectiveness and the efficiency of the algorithms.

However, unfortunately, none of the existing algorithms satisfy the following criteria at the same time that should be met for their practical use: _1) scalable to hyperparameter dimension, 2) online optimization, 3) memory-efficient, 4) avoid short-horizon bias_ . Please See Table 1 for the comparison of existing gradient-based HO algorithms in the above four criteria.

Forward-Mode Differentiation (FMD) (Franceschi et al., 2017) in Table 1 is an algorithm that forward-propagates Jacobians (i.e. derivatives of the update function) from the first to the last step, which is analogous to real-time recurrent learning (RTRL) (Williams & Zipser, 1989) in recurrent neural networks. FMD allows online optimization (i.e. update hyperparameters every inner-step)

1

with the intermediate Jacobians and also computes the hypergradients over the entire horizon. However, a critical limitation is that the time and space complexity linearly increases w.r.t. the hyperparameter dimension. Thus, we cannot use FMD for solving many practical meta-learning problems that come with millions of hyperparameters, which is the main problem we tackle in this paper.

Secondly, Reverse-Mode Differentiation (RMD) (Maclaurin et al., 2015) back-propagates the Jacobian-vector products (JVPs) from the last to the initial step, which is structurally identical to backprop through time (BPTT) (Werbos, 1990). RMD is scalable to the hyperparameter dimension, but the space complexity linearly increases w.r.t. the horizon length (i.e., the number of innergradient steps used to compute the hypergradient). It is possible to reduce the memory burden by checkpointing some of the previous weights and further interpolating between the weights to approximate the trajectory (Fu et al., 2016). However, RMD and its variants are not scalable for online optimization. This is because they do not retain the intermediate Jacobians unlike FMD and thus need to recompute the whole second-order term for every online HO step.

Thirdly, algorithms based on Implicit Function Theorem (IFT) are applicable to high-dimensional HO (Bengio, 2000; Pedregosa, 2016). Under the assumption that the main model parameters have arrived at convergence, the best-response Jacobian, i.e. how the converged model parameters change w.r.t. the hyperparameters, can be expressed by only the information available at the last step, such as the inverse of Hessian at convergence. Thus, we do not have to explicitly unroll the previous update steps. Due to the heavy cost of computing inverse-Hessian-vector product, Lorraine et al. (2020) propose to approximate it by an iterative method, which works well for high-dimensional HO problems. However, still it is not straightforward to use the method for online optimization because of the convergence assumption. That is, computing hypergradients before convergence does not guarantee the quality of the hypergradients.

To our knowledge, the short horizon approximation such as one-step lookahead (1-step in Table 1) (Luketina et al., 2016) is the only existing method that _fully_ supports online optimization, while being scalable to the hyperparameter dimension at the same time. It computes hypergradients only over a single update step and ignores the past learning trajectory, which is computationally efficient as only a single JVP is computed per each online HO step. However, this approximation suffers from the short horizon bias (Wu et al., 2018) by definition.

In this paper, we propose a novel HO algorithm that can simultaneously satisfy all the aforementioned criteria for practical HO. The key idea is to distill the entire second-order term into a single JVP. As a result, we only need to compute the single JVP for each online HO step, and at the same time the distilled JVP can consider longer horizons than short horizon approximations such as one-step lookahead or first-order method. We summarize the contribution of this paper as follows:

- We propose _HyperDistill_ , a novel HO algorithm that satisfies the aforementioned four criteria for practical HO, each of which is crucial for a HO algorithm to be applied to the current meta-learning frameworks.

- We show how to efficiently distill the hypergradient second-order term into a single JVP.

- We empirically demonstrate that our algorithm converges faster and provides better generalization performance at convergence, with three recent meta-learning models and on two benchmark image datasets.

## 2 RELATED WORK

**Hyperparameter optimization** When the hyperparameter dimension is small (e.g. less than 100), random search (Bergstra & Bengio, 2012) or Bayesian optimization (Snoek et al., 2012) works well. However, when the hyperparameter is high-dimensional, gradient-based HO is often preferred since random or Bayesian search could become infeasible. One of the most well known methods for gradient-based HO are based on Implicit Function Theorem which compute or approximate the inverse Hessian only at convergence. Bengio (2000) computes the exact inverse Hessian, and Luketina et al. (2016) approximate the inverse Hessian with the identity matrix, which is identical to the onestep lookahead approximation. Pedregosa (2016) approximates the inverse Hessian with conjugate gradients (CG) method. Lorraine et al. (2020) propose Neumann approximation, which is numerically more stable than CG approximation. On the other hand, Domke (2012) proposes unrolled differentiation for solving bi-level optimization, and Shaban et al. (2019) analyzes the truncated unrolled differentiation, which is computationally more efficient. Unrolled diffrentiation can be fur-

ther categorized into forward (FMD) and reverse mode (RMD) (Franceschi et al., 2017). FMD is more suitable for optimizing low-dimensional hyperparamters (Im et al., 2021; Micaelli & Storkey, 2020), but RMD is more scalable to the hyperparameter dimension. Maclaurin et al. (2015) proposes a more memory-efficient RMD, which reverses the SGD trajectory with momentum. Fu et al. (2016) further reduce memory burden of RMD by approximating the learning trajectory with linear interpolation. Luketina et al. (2016) can also be understood as a short horizon approximation of RMD for online optimization. Our method also supports online optimization, but the critical difference is that our algorithm can alleviate the short horizon bias (Wu et al., 2018). RMD is basically a type of backpropagation and it is available in deep learning libraries (Grefenstette et al., 2019).

**Meta-learning** Meta-learning (Schmidhuber, 1987; Thrun & Pratt, 1998) aims to learn a model that generalizes over a distribution of tasks (Vinyals et al., 2016; Ravi & Larochelle, 2016). While there exists a variety of approaches, in this paper we focus on gradient-based meta-learning (Finn et al., 2017), especially the methods with high-dimensional hyperparameters that do not participate in inner-optimization. For instance, there have been many attempts to precondition the innergradients for faster inner-optimization, either by _warping_ the parameter space with every pair of consecutive layers interleaved with a warp layer (Lee & Choi, 2018; Flennerhag et al., 2019) or directly modulating the inner-gradients with diagonal (Li et al., 2017) or block-diagonal matrix (Park & Oliva, 2019). Perturbation function is another form of hyperparameters that help the inner-learner generalize better (Lee et al., 2019; Ryu et al., 2020; Tseng et al., 2020). It is also possible to let the whole feature extractor be hyperparameters and only adapt the last fully-connected layer (Raghu et al., 2019). On the other hand, some of the meta-learning literatures do not assume a task distribution, but tune their hyperparameters with a holdouot validation set, similarly to the conventional HO setting. In this case, the one-step lookahead method (Luketina et al., 2016) is mostly used for scalable online HO, in context of domain generalization (Li et al., 2018), handling class imbalance (Ren et al., 2018; Shu et al., 2019), gradient-based neural architecture search (Liu et al., 2018), and coefficient of norm-based regularizer (Balaji et al., 2018). Although we mainly focus on metalearning setting in this work, whose goal is to transfer knowledge through a task distribution, it is straightforward to apply our method to conventional HO problems.

## 3 BACKGROUND

In this section, we first introduce RMD and its approximations for efficient computation. We then introduce our novel algorithm that supports high-dimensional online HO over the entire horizon.

## 3.2 REVERSE-MODE DIFFERENTIATION AND ITS APPROXIMATIONS

Basically, RMD is structurally analogous to backpropagation through time (BPTT) (Werbos, 1990). In RMD, we first obtain _αT_ = _[∂][L]_[val] _∂w_[(] _[w] T[T][ ,][λ]_[)] and back-propagate _A_ and _B_ from the last to the first step in the form of JVPs (See Algorithm 1). Whereas RMD is much faster than FMD as we only need to compute one or two JVPs per each step, it usually requires to store all the previous weights _w_ 0 _, . . . , wT −_ 1 to compute the previous-step JVPs, unless we consider reversible training with momentum optimizer (Maclaurin et al., 2015). Therefore, when _w_ is high-dimensional, RMD is only applicable to short-horizon problems such as few-shot learning (e.g. _T_ = 5 in Finn et al. (2017)). **Trajectory approximation.** Instead of storing all the previous weights for computing _A_ and _B_ , we can approximate the learning trajectory by linearly interpolating between the last weight _wT_ and the initial weight _w_ 0. Algorithm 2 illustrates the procedure called DrMAD (Fu et al., 2016), where each ˆ intermediate weight _wt_ is approximated by _wt_ = �1 _− T[t]_ � _w_ 0 + _T[t][w][T]_[for] _[ t]_[ = 1] _[, . . . , T][−]_[1][.] _[A]_[ and] _B_ are also approximated by _A_[ˆ] _s_ = _[∂]_[Φ][(][ ˆ] _w∂sw_ ˆ _−s_ 1 _−,λ_ 1; _Ds_ ) and _B_[ˆ] _t_ = _[∂]_[Φ][(] _w_[ ˆ] _t−∂λ_ 1 _,λ_ ; _Dt_ ) , respectively. However, although DrMAD dramatically lower the space complexity, it does not reduce the number of JVPs per each hypergradient step. **Short-horizon approximations.** One-step lookahead approximation (Luketina et al., 2016) is currently one of the most popular high-dimensional online HO method that can avoid computing the excessive number of JVPs (Li et al., 2018; Ren et al., 2018; Shu et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2018; Balaji et al., 2018). The idea is very simple; for each online HO step we only care about the last previous step and ignore the rest of the learning trajectory for computational efficiency. Specifically, for each step _t_ = 1 _, . . . , T_ we compute the hypergradient by viewing _wt−_ 1 as constant, which yields _dwdλt[≈][∂w] ∂λ[t]_ �� _wt−_ 1[=] _[B][t]_[(See][Eq.][(][3][)).][Or,][we][may][completely][ignore][all][the][second-order][deriva-] tives for computational efficiency, such that _[dw] dλ[t][≈]_[0][(][Flennerhag][et][al.][,][2019][;][Ryu][et][al.][,][2020][).] While those approximations enable online HO with low cost, they are intrinsically vulnerable to short-horizon bias (Wu et al., 2018) by definition.

## 5 EXPERIMENTS

**Baselines.** We demonstrate the efficacy of our algorithm by comparing to the following baselines. **1) First-Order Approximation (FO).** Computationally the most efficient HO algorithm that completely ignores the second-order term, i.e. _g_[SO] = 0. **2) One-step Look-ahead Approximation (1-step). (Luketina et al., 2016)** The short-horizon approximation where only a single step is unrolled to compute each hypergradient. **3) DrMAD. (Fu et al., 2016)** An approximation of RMD that linearly interpolates between the initial and the last weight to save memory (see Algorithm 2). **4) Neumann IFT (N.IFT). (Lorraine et al., 2020)** An IFT based method that approximates the inverse-Hessian-vector product by Neumann series. Note that this method supports online optimization around convergence. Specifically, among total _T_ = 100 inner-steps, N.IFT( _N, K_ ) means for the last _K_ steps we perform online HO each with _N_ inversion steps. It requires total ( _N_ + 1) _× K_ JVPs. We tune ( _N, K_ ) among _{_ (2 _,_ 25) _,_ (5 _,_ 10) _,_ (10 _,_ 5) _}_ , roughly computing 50 JVPs per inner-opt. **5) HyperDistill.** Our high-dimensional online HO algorithm based on the idea of knowledge distillation. We tune the decaying factor within _γ ∈{_ 0 _._ 9 _,_ 0 _._ 99 _,_ 0 _._ 999 _,_ 0 _._ 9999 _}_ . The linear regression is done every 50 inner-optimization problems.

**Target meta-learning models.** We test on the following three meta-learning models. **1) Almost No Inner Loop (ANIL). (Raghu et al., 2019)** The intuition of ANIL is that the need for task-specific adaptation diminishes when the task distribution is homogeneous. Following this intuition, based on a typical 4-layer convolutional network with 32 channels (Finn et al., 2017), we designate the three bottom layers as the high-dimensional hyperparameter and the 4th convolutional layer and the last fully connected layer as the weight, similarly to Javed & White (2019).

**Use of Reptile.** Note that for all the above meta-learning models, we meta-learn the weight initialization with Reptile (Nichol et al., 2018) as well, representing a more practical meta-learning scenario than learning from random initialization. To generate each task, we randomly sample 10 classes from each dataset (5000 examples) and randomly split them into 2500 training and 2500 test examples. **1) TinyImageNet.** (Le & Yang, 2015) This dataset contains 200 classes of general categories. We split them into 100, 40, and 60 classes for meta-training, meta-validation, and meta-test. Each class has 500 examples of size 64 _×_ 64. **2) CIFAR100.** (Krizhevsky et al., 2009) This dataset contains 100 classes of general categories. We split them into 50, 20, and 30 classes for meta-training, meta-validation, and meta-test. Each class has 500 examples of size 32 _×_ 32.

We repeat this over 5 different meta-training runs and report mean and 95% confidence intervals (see Table 2). **Code is publicly available at:** https://github.com/haebeom-lee/hyperdistill

We perform the following analysis together with the WarpGrad model and CIFAR100 dataset. **HyperDistill provides faster convergence and better generalization.** Figure 2 shows that HyperDistill shows much faster meta-training convergence than the baselines for all the meta-learning

We see that the convergence of offline method such as DrMAD is significantly worse than a simple first-order method, demonstrating the importance of frequent update via online optimization. HyperDistill shows significantly better convergence than FO and 1-step because it is online and at the same time alleviates the short horizon bias. As a result, Table 2 shows that the meta-test performance of HyperDistill is significantly better than the baselines, although it requires comparable number of JVPs per each inner-optimiztion.

**HyperDistill is a reasonable approximation of the true hypergradient.** We see from Figure 3(a) that the hypergradient obtained from HyperDistill is more similar to the exact RMD than those obtained from FO and 1-step, demonstrating that HyperDistill can actually alleviate the short horizon bias. HyperDistill is even comparable to N.IFT(10 _,_ 1) that computes 11 JVPs, whereas HyperDistill computes only a single JVP. Such results indicate that the approximation we used in Eq. (7) and DrMAD in Eq. (16) are accurate enough. Figure 3(b) shows that with careful tuning of _γ_ (e.g. 0 _._ 99), the direction of the approximated second-order term in Eq. Note that wall-clock time is roughly proportional to the number of JVPs per inner-optimization. In Appendix F, we can see that the actual increase in memory cost and wallcock time is very marginal compared to 1-step approximation.

## 6 CONCLUSION

In this work, we proposed a novel HO method, _HyperDistill_ , that can optimize high-dimensional hyperparameters in an online manner. It was done by approximating the exact second-order term with knowledge distillation. We demonstrated that HyperDistill provides faster meta-convergence and better generalization performance based on realistic meta-learning methods and datasets. We also verified that it is thanks to the accurate approximations we proposed.

progressive__claude-opus-4.7.overall_feedback:
## High-Level Feedback

This paper tackles a genuinely important and well-motivated problem: existing gradient-based hyperparameter optimization (HO) methods each fail to simultaneously satisfy the practical desiderata of scalability, online optimization, memory efficiency, and avoidance of short-horizon bias. The framing via Table 1 is effective and immediately clarifies the paper's contribution niche, and the proposed idea—distilling the expensive second-order term into a single Jacobian-vector product (JVP) via knowledge distillation—is intuitive and potentially impactful for large-scale meta-learning. The writing in the introduction is generally clear and the motivation flows logically through the limitations of FMD, RMD, IFT, and one-step lookahead methods. However, several concerns arise even from this opening. First, the core novelty hinges on a distillation approximation whose fidelity and bias properties are not previewed theoretically; the reader is left wondering under what conditions a single JVP can faithfully capture a full unrolled second-order term, and whether this simply reintroduces a different form of short-horizon or approximation bias rather than eliminating it. Second, the claim that the method "avoids short-horizon bias" while using only a single JVP per step deserves careful justification, as this appears to be the paper's most contestable assertion. Third, the characterization of IFT methods as only partially supporting online optimization (the "△" in Table 1) is somewhat under-argued and could be contested by proponents of those methods. Finally, the empirical validation is described only briefly ("two meta-learning methods and three benchmark datasets"), and given that the paper competes against a crowded landscape of HO methods, the strength of the contribution will rest heavily on whether experiments include rigorous comparisons (including wall-clock time, memory, and generalization) against all baselines in Table 1, along with ablations isolating the benefit of distillation versus simply using longer horizons. Overall, the paper addresses a timely problem with a creative solution, but its significance depends on theoretical grounding of the distillation approximation and comprehensive empirical evidence that will need close scrutiny in the remainder of the paper.

comments.title: Distance metric on datasets is not defined at the point the Lipschitz assumption is introduced
comments.explanation: The Lipschitz inequality in Eq. (9) uses ||(w,D) - (w_{i-1}, D_i)||_X but ||D||_2 for a dataset D is not a standard notion. The authors later (in the text after Eq. (13)) say they 'simply interpret p_t and 1-p_t as probabilities with which we proportionally subsample each dataset' and acknowledge 'it is not straightforward to think of Euclidean distance between datasets.' So they are aware of this gap but proceed anyway by analogy. The derivation leading to Eq. (12)/(13) relies on a formal Euclidean structure that doesn't actually exist for datasets, which is a real theoretical gap but is addressed heuristically in the subsampling procedure.

comments.title: Inconsistency between p_t definition in Eq. (13) vs. Eq. (17)
comments.quote: Denoting _ps_ = (1 _− γ[s][−]_[1] ) _/_ (1 _− γ[s]_ ), we have
comments.explanation: Earlier in Section 4.2, the sequential update for w_t* uses p_t = (γ − γ^t)/(1 − γ^t) ∈ [0,1). Now in Section 4.3, for the linear estimator's distilled JVP v_s, the authors denote p_s = (1 − γ^{s−1})/(1 − γ^s). These two expressions are different, and the authors reuse the letter p but with different meaning/formula across the two sections. A careful reader could be confused: is this a typo or an intentional different weighting? Since for the linear estimator one is computing w_s* over a horizon ending at step s (not T), the weighting scheme could legitimately differ, but the text does not justify why the form changes.

comments.title: N.IFT JVP-count claim appears to not match stated tuning range
comments.quote: We tune ( _N, K_ ) among _{_ (2 _,_ 25) _,_ (5 _,_ 10) _,_ (10 _,_ 5) _}_ , roughly computing 50 JVPs per inner-opt.
comments.explanation: The text states total JVPs = (N+1)×K. For (2,25): 3×25=75; for (5,10): 6×10=60; for (10,5): 11×5=55. These totals are over the T=100 inner-steps (i.e., the last K inner-steps only). The claim 'roughly computing 50 JVPs per inner-opt' is confusing — 55-75 JVPs total divided over the K online HO steps would give ~11 per HO step, not 50. Table 2 reports '# JVPs / inner-opt' of '{55,60,75}' for Neumann IFT, suggesting the authors are reporting the total JVPs across the whole trajectory as if they were 'per inner-opt', which is inconsistent with the header. A reader could reasonably be misled about the per-step computational cost of N.IFT.

comments.title: Lipschitz assumption on f_t questionable given σ normalization
comments.explanation: The function f_t includes normalization σ(x) = x/||x||_2, which is not Lipschitz continuous near the origin (the normalization map is discontinuous at 0). So the Lipschitz assumption may be problematic in regions where α_t·B_i is near zero. This is a real technical concern for the derivation since the subsequent lower bound relies on this inequality.

comments.title: Hessian identity approximation glosses over sign/magnitude constraints
comments.explanation: The derivation sets γ = 1 - η^Inner · k. For γ ≥ 0 requires η^Inner · k ≤ 1, and for the series sum to be well-defined we'd typically want |γ| < 1. The paper doesn't discuss these constraints explicitly. γ is later used in a range [0,1) (via p_t = (γ-γ^t)/(1-γ^t)), so effectively they need γ ∈ [0,1). This is a mild underspecification.

comments.title: Total JVP count for DrMAD online optimization needs clarification
comments.quote: For each online HO step _t_ = 1 _, . . . , T_ we need to compute 2 _t −_ 1 JVPs, thus the number of total JVPs to complete a single trajectory accumulates up to[] _[T] t_ =1[(2] _[t][−]_[1) =] _[ T]_[ 2]
comments.explanation: The claim that DrMAD requires 2t-1 JVPs per online HO step t is not immediately obvious from Algorithm 2, which for a single hypergradient computation at horizon T does a backward loop with 2 JVPs per step (~2T JVPs per hypergradient call). The count 2t-1 implicitly assumes each online step runs DrMAD from scratch with horizon t. This interpretation is implicit and the reader needs to infer it; the arithmetic does work out to T^2 under this reading, but the reasoning deserves explicit justification.

comments.title: IFT online optimization marked with triangle without explanation in table caption
comments.quote: |Online opt.|O|X|X|**_△_**|O|**O**|
comments.explanation: The table uses a triangle (△) symbol for IFT under 'Online opt.' but the legend/caption does not define what the triangle means versus O or X. The text later explains 'it is not straightforward to use the method for online optimization because of the convergence assumption,' which suggests △ means 'partially' or 'with caveats.' A reader encountering the table first would have to infer the meaning.

comments.title: Setting π* = 1 for MetaWeightNet justified only informally
comments.quote: Also, in this meta model the direct gradient is zero, _g_[FO] = 0. In this case, _π[∗]_ in HyperDistill has a meaning of nothing but rescaling the learning rate, so we simply set _π[∗]_ = 1.
comments.explanation: The argument is that when g^FO=0, the hypergradient is just π* f_t(w*,D*), and since f_t is unit-normalized, the scalar π* is absorbable into the hyperparameter learning rate. This glosses over that π* could still vary across HO steps t, which would be different from a constant learning rate rescaling — if π*_t varies per step, fixing it to 1 changes the effective per-step learning rate schedule.

comments.title: Claim that HyperDistill computes 'only a single JVP' glosses over DrMAD cost
comments.quote: HyperDistill is even comparable to N.IFT(10 _,_ 1) that computes 11 JVPs, whereas HyperDistill computes only a single JVP.
comments.explanation: HyperDistill also runs a DrMAD-based backward pass periodically (the LinearEstimation procedure) to fit θ, which itself requires multiple JVPs. The authors defend this by saying LinearEstimation runs infrequently (every ~50 inner-opts), so amortized per-step cost is close to one JVP. The phrasing 'only a single JVP' is accurate for the per-step hyperparameter update cost but could mislead a reader into thinking no other JVPs are ever computed.

comments.title: Table 1 claim about FMD horizon inconsistent with memory claim
comments.quote: |Horizon_>_1|O|O|O|O|X|**O**|
comments.explanation: The table claims FMD supports Horizon>1 and has constant memory. However, the text explicitly states for FMD that 'the time and space complexity linearly increases w.r.t. the hyperparameter dimension' — this is about hyperparameter dimension, not horizon, so it's consistent with constant memory w.r.t. horizon. But standard knowledge of FMD/RTRL is that memory is O(|w|·|λ|) — constant w.r.t. horizon T but not w.r.t. λ. The 'Constant memory' row marking FMD as O is technically correct only if interpreted as constant in T. This is a minor ambiguity: a reader could misread the table as saying FMD has fully constant memory, contradicting the High-dim. X entry. The two entries together are consistent only under the implicit interpretation that 'constant memory' means constant w.r.t. horizon length. The table caption does not clarify this.

comments.title: IFT online optimization marked with triangle without explanation in intro
comments.quote: |Online opt.|O|X|X|**_△_**|O|**O**|
comments.explanation: The table uses a triangle (△) symbol for IFT under 'Online opt.' but the legend/caption does not define what the triangle means versus O or X. The text later explains 'it is not straightforward to use the method for online optimization because of the convergence assumption,' which suggests △ means 'partially' or 'with caveats.' A reader encountering the table first would have to infer the meaning. This is a minor presentation concern, not a technical error.

comments.title: DrMAD algorithm uses approximated Jacobians without explicit definition in algorithm box
comments.explanation: The DrMAD algorithm (Algorithm 2) uses $\hat{A}_t$ and $\hat{B}_t$ without defining them in the algorithm box itself. Reading the surrounding text, I found that these are defined later in Section 3.2: $\hat{A}_s = \partial\Phi(\hat{w}_{s-1},\lambda; D_s)/\partial \hat{w}_{s-1}$ and $\hat{B}_t = \partial\Phi(\hat{w}_{t-1},\lambda; D_t)/\partial\lambda$. So this is a minor forward-reference issue rather than a real error — the definitions are provided in the next subsection. Not a flag-worthy issue on its own.

comments.title: Claim that Luketina et al. (2016) is identical to one-step lookahead via identity inverse Hessian
comments.quote: Luketina et al. (2016) approximate the inverse Hessian with the identity matrix, which is identical to the onestep lookahead approximation.
comments.explanation: Initially this gave me pause: the paper uses 'one-step lookahead' throughout to mean the short-horizon RMD with $dw_t/d\lambda \approx B_t$ (treating $w_{t-1}$ as constant), which is derived by truncating the RMD recursion, not by approximating an inverse Hessian in IFT. Here in Related Work, the authors equate 'approximating inverse Hessian with identity' (an IFT-style approximation) with the one-step lookahead. In fact these two derivations yield the same formula for the hypergradient update when the inner optimizer is vanilla SGD with unit learning rate (since $A_j = I - \eta H \approx I$ when $H \approx 0$, which matches the identity inverse-Hessian approximation up to scaling). This is a known equivalence in the HO literature, so the claim is defensible. Minor framing concern only — a careful reader might want a brief justification, but it's a Related Work section.

comments.title: Total JVP count for DrMAD online optimization appears inconsistent
comments.quote: For each online HO step _t_ = 1 _, . . . , T_ we need to compute 2 _t −_ 1 JVPs, thus the number of total JVPs to complete a single trajectory accumulates up to[] _[T] t_ =1[(2] _[t][−]_[1) =] _[ T]_[ 2]
comments.explanation: I initially wanted to check the arithmetic: sum_{t=1}^T (2t-1) = 2·T(T+1)/2 - T = T^2+T-T = T^2. That matches. However, the claim that DrMAD requires 2t-1 JVPs per online HO step t is questionable. Looking at Algorithm 2, for a single hypergradient computation at horizon T, DrMAD does a backward loop with 2 JVPs per step (computing α B̂_t and updating α ← α Â_t), so roughly 2T JVPs per hypergradient call, not 2t-1. The count 2t-1 seems to be treating each online step as running DrMAD from scratch with horizon t (so 2t JVPs minus one for the last-step α update that isn't needed), which is a reasonable interpretation if one wants online HO at each step t=1,...,T. This interpretation is implicit and the reader needs to infer it; it is a minor ambiguity but the arithmetic does work out to T^2 under this reading.

comments.title: Lipschitz assumption on f_t with γ=0 is questionable given σ normalization
comments.explanation: The function f_t includes normalization σ(x) = x/||x||_2, which is not Lipschitz continuous near the origin (the normalization map is discontinuous at 0). So the Lipschitz assumption may be problematic in regions where α_t·B_i is near zero. This is a real technical concern for the derivation since the subsequent lower bound relies on this inequality. However, authors are making a working assumption and this kind of smoothness assumption is common in the literature for deriving approximation bounds, so I'd rate this as minor rather than a blocking issue.

comments.title: Hessian identity approximation claim glosses over sign/magnitude issues
comments.explanation: The derivation sets γ = 1 - η^Inner · k. For this to satisfy γ ≥ 0 (as stated) requires η^Inner · k ≤ 1, and for the series sum to be well-defined/small we'd typically want |γ| < 1. If k is the largest Hessian eigenvalue or similar, this is the standard stability condition, but the paper doesn't discuss these constraints. Also, γ is later used in a range [0,1) (via p_t = (γ-γ^t)/(1-γ^t)), so effectively they need γ ∈ [0,1). This is a mild underspecification but the reader familiar with gradient descent stability can fill in the gap.

comments.title: Distance metric on datasets is not defined at the point the Lipschitz assumption is introduced
comments.explanation: The Lipschitz inequality in Eq. (9) uses ||(w,D) - (w_{i-1}, D_i)||_X but ||D||_2 for a dataset D is not a standard notion. The authors later (in the text after Eq. (13)) say they 'simply interpret p_t and 1-p_t as probabilities with which we proportionally subsample each dataset' and acknowledge 'it is not straightforward to think of Euclidean distance between datasets.' So they are aware of this gap but proceed anyway by analogy. The derivation leading to Eq. (12)/(13) relies on a formal Euclidean structure that doesn't actually exist for datasets, which is a real theoretical gap but is addressed heuristically in the subsampling procedure. Worth flagging as a moderate gap in rigor.

comments.title: Inconsistency between p_t definition in Eq. (13) vs. Eq. (17)
comments.quote: Denoting _ps_ = (1 _− γ[s][−]_[1] ) _/_ (1 _− γ[s]_ ), we have
comments.explanation: Earlier in Section 4.2, the sequential update for w_t* uses p_t = (γ − γ^t)/(1 − γ^t) ∈ [0,1). Now in Section 4.3, for the linear estimator's distilled JVP v_s, the authors denote p_s = (1 − γ^{s−1})/(1 − γ^s). These two expressions are different (the first is (γ − γ^t)/(1 − γ^t), the second is (1 − γ^{s−1})/(1 − γ^s)). The authors reuse the letter p but with different meaning/formula across the two sections, and they say 'similarly to Eq. (13) and (14)' which suggests the reader should interpret them analogously. A careful reader could be confused: is this a typo or an intentional different weighting? Since for the linear estimator one is computing w_s* over a horizon ending at step s (not T), the weighting scheme could legitimately differ, but the text does not justify why the form changes. This is a moderate notation/consistency concern that would hinder reproducibility.

comments.title: Algorithm 3/4 pseudocode is severely garbled
comments.explanation: The pseudocode for Algorithms 3 and 4 as rendered here is essentially unreadable — line numbers are interleaved from two columns, variable names are split across bracket markers, and assignments are concatenated in ways that do not correspond to valid algorithmic steps. While this is partly an OCR artifact of two-column pseudocode being merged, the result is that a reader trying to reproduce the method from this passage alone cannot actually determine the order of operations, which quantities are computed when, or what is returned. Since Algorithms 3 and 4 are the core practical contribution, this is a reproducibility concern — though likely the original PDF is clear, so I'd flag this as a note to the authors/publishers to verify the layout. Marking moderate because readers may legitimately fail to reconstruct the algorithm from the rendering shown.

comments.title: Linear estimator c_γ(t;θ) form not shown in the provided passage
comments.quote: Based on the observation that the form of lower bound in Eq. (][10][) is] _[ roughly]_ proportional to[] _[t] i_ =1 _[δ][t,i]_[=][ ] _[t] i_ =1 _[γ][t][−][i][ ∥][α][t][B][i][∥]_ 2[, we conveniently set] _[ c][γ]_[(] _[t]_[;] _[ θ]_[)][ to as follows:]
comments.explanation: The authors promise to define c_γ(t;θ) 'as follows' but the actual formula is not rendered in the passage (likely Eq. 15 which OCR dropped). Downstream, they use x_s = ||v_s||_2 · (1−γ)/(1−γ^s), which suggests c_γ(t;θ) = θ · ||v_t||_2 · (1−γ)/(1−γ^t) or similar. Since the paper later references this, the omission is due to OCR rather than author error, and readers would have the original PDF. No actionable issue against the authors — flagging only because the linear estimator's form is central to the method and the reader of this extract cannot verify the consistency between the stated proportionality and the chosen parametric form. Per instructions, I should not over-flag missing equations that are referenced but not shown.

comments.title: Claim that γ=0 recovers one-step lookahead needs checking
comments.quote: our method is a generalization of the one-step lookahead approximation, i.e. _γ_ = 0, which yields _wt[∗]_[=] _[w][t][−]_[1][and] _[ D] t[∗]_[=] _[D][t]_[, ignoring the whole] information about the past learning trajectory except the last step.
comments.explanation: Let me verify this. Eq. (13) uses p_t = (γ − γ^t)/(1 − γ^t). At γ=0 and t≥1: p_t = (0 − 0)/(1−0) = 0 (for t≥2; for t=1 the formula gives 0/1 = 0 as well, but the t=1 base case is D_1* ← D_1 and presumably w_1* ← w_0 or w_1). With p_t = 0, the sequential update w_t* ← p_t w_{t-1}* + (1−p_t) w_{t-1} = w_{t-1}, which indeed matches one-step lookahead. Similarly D_t* = SS(D_t*,0) ∪ SS(D_t,1) = D_t. So the claim checks out. However, the authors earlier established that one-step lookahead uses dw_t/dλ ≈ B_t = ∂Φ(w,λ;D)/∂λ at (w_{t-1},D_t), which would correspond to f_t evaluated at (w_{t-1},D_t). This is consistent. No issue — the claim is correct.

comments.title: Inconsistent learning rate symbol (μ vs η)
comments.quote: set the learning rate _µ_[Inner] = 0 _._ 1 for MetaWeightNet and _µ_[Inner] = 0 _._ 01 for the others
comments.explanation: Earlier the paper and algorithms use η^Inner and η^Hyper (and η^Reptile) to denote learning rates (e.g., 'vanilla SGD with learning rate η^Inner'). In the experimental setup, the authors suddenly switch to μ^Inner and μ^Hyper. This could be an OCR misread of η as μ, but given it appears consistently across multiple mentions in the experimental section and the values are specified, it's likely the authors genuinely switched notation. Either way, it's a minor notational inconsistency that a careful reader would notice but easily resolve.

comments.title: N.IFT JVP-count claim appears to not match stated tuning range
comments.quote: We tune ( _N, K_ ) among _{_ (2 _,_ 25) _,_ (5 _,_ 10) _,_ (10 _,_ 5) _}_ , roughly computing 50 JVPs per inner-opt.
comments.explanation: The text states total JVPs = (N+1)×K. For (2,25): 3×25=75; for (5,10): 6×10=60; for (10,5): 11×5=55. These totals are over the T=100 inner-steps (i.e., the last K inner-steps only). The claim 'roughly computing 50 JVPs per inner-opt' is confusing — 55-75 JVPs total divided over the K online HO steps would give ~11 per HO step, not 50. Looking at Table 2, which reports '# JVPs / inner-opt' of '{55,60,75}' for Neumann IFT, it seems the authors are reporting the total JVPs across the whole trajectory as if they were 'per inner-opt', which is inconsistent with the header. A reader could reasonably be misled about the per-step computational cost of N.IFT. The numbers 55/60/75 match the totals above, so the table header label '# JVPs / inner-opt' is misleading: these are totals, not per-inner-step counts. The 'roughly 50' phrase in the prose is also loose.

comments.title: Setting π* = 1 for MetaWeightNet justified only informally
comments.quote: Also, in this meta model the direct gradient is zero, _g_[FO] = 0. In this case, _π[∗]_ in HyperDistill has a meaning of nothing but rescaling the learning rate, so we simply set _π[∗]_ = 1.
comments.explanation: The argument is that when g^FO=0, the hypergradient is just π* f_t(w*,D*), and since f_t is unit-normalized (by definition σ(·) = x/||x||_2), the scalar π* is absorbable into the hyperparameter learning rate. This is plausible but glosses over that π* could still vary across HO steps t, which would be different from a constant learning rate rescaling — if π*_t varies per step, fixing it to 1 changes the effective per-step learning rate schedule. The justification is informal but defensible given that the hyperparameter learning rate is separately tuned; flagging as minor since a careful reader can reconstruct the reasoning.

comments.title: Figure 3 caption contains repeated/garbled text
comments.quote: The curves in **(b)** correspond to Eq. (7) with various7) with various) with various _γ_ .
comments.explanation: The caption text 'with various7) with various) with various' is clearly a duplicated/garbled fragment, likely an OCR or typesetting artifact. While the intent is clear (curves correspond to Eq. (7) with various γ), this is a formatting artifact I'm instructed not to flag. However, it's worth noting because if this is actually in the published text, it would be a real typo. Given the instruction to ignore formatting issues, I'll note it as minor.

comments.title: Claim that HyperDistill computes 'only a single JVP' glosses over DrMAD cost
comments.quote: HyperDistill is even comparable to N.IFT(10 _,_ 1) that computes 11 JVPs, whereas HyperDistill computes only a single JVP.
comments.explanation: Initially this seemed like a clean comparison. However, in the summary and Algorithm 3, HyperDistill also runs a DrMAD-based backward pass periodically (the LinearEstimation procedure) to fit θ, which itself requires multiple JVPs (one or two per horizon step s = 1,...,T). The authors do defend this by saying LinearEstimation runs infrequently (every ~50 inner-opts), so amortized per-step cost is close to one JVP. The phrasing 'only a single JVP' is accurate for the per-step hyperparameter update cost but could mislead a reader into thinking no other JVPs are ever computed. The Figure 4(e) comparison on 'JVPs per inner-opt' presumably amortizes this correctly, but the prose here is somewhat glossing. Minor since context (Algorithm 3, summary) makes the full picture available.

comments.title: Typo: 'compuatationally'
comments.quote: **HyperDistill is compuatationally efficient.**
comments.explanation: Typo 'compuatationally' for 'computationally'. This is a formatting/typo issue which I'm instructed not to flag, but flagging at minor severity since it is in a section heading of the text.

progressive__gpt-5.5.label: Progressive (gpt-5.5)
progressive__gpt-5.5.overall_feedback: The paper addresses an important and practically relevant limitation of gradient-based hyperparameter optimization for meta-learning: obtaining online, memory-efficient hypergradients for high-dimensional hyperparameters without incurring severe short-horizon bias. The motivation is clear, the taxonomy of existing methods is helpful, and the proposed idea of distilling a long-horizon second-order term into a single Jacobian-vector product appears conceptually interesting and potentially impactful. That said, the beginning would benefit from sharper technical precision and clearer positioning: several claims in Table 1 and the surrounding discussion are somewhat coarse-grained, and the distinctions between “online optimization,” “constant memory,” and “horizon > 1” need more formal definitions to avoid ambiguity. The central mechanism—how a second-order term can be reliably distilled, what target is used, and why this does not simply move the computational burden elsewhere—is only sketched at a high level, so the reader is left wanting an early intuitive explanation of the approximation, its assumptions, and possible failure modes. The introduction also occasionally overstates the universality of the proposed criteria and the limitations of prior work; a more nuanced discussion of trade-offs, approximation error, and computational overhead would strengthen the framing. Overall, the paper appears promising and well-motivated, with a potentially strong contribution, but its quality will depend heavily on whether the later sections provide rigorous derivation, convincing evidence that the distillation target is stable and efficient, and fair empirical comparisons against strong unrolled, implicit, and short-horizon baselines.

comments.title: Experimental scope is stated inconsistently
comments.quote: We demonstrate the effectiveness of our method on two different meta-learning methods and three benchmark datasets.
comments.explanation: The abstract says the method is evaluated on two meta-learning methods and three datasets, while the introduction later claims experiments with three recent meta-learning models and two benchmark image datasets. These statements swap both the method/model count and the dataset count. The issue is easy to fix by aligning both claims with the actual experimental setup, but as written the empirical scope is unclear.

comments.title: Reverse-mode products are repeatedly described as JVPs rather than VJPs
comments.quote: In RMD, we first obtain _αT_ = _[∂][L]_[val] _∂w_[(] _[w] T[T][ ,][λ]_[)] and back-propagate _A_ and _B_ from the last to the first step in the form of JVPs (See Algorithm 1). Whereas RMD is much faster than FMD as we only need to compute one or two JVPs per each step
comments.explanation: Algorithm 1 propagates quantities such as αA_t and αB_t, with α acting as a cotangent/row adjoint. In standard automatic-differentiation terminology these are vector-Jacobian products, or reverse-mode pullbacks, not Jacobian-vector products. A JVP would have the form A_t v or B_t v with a tangent vector. The formulas make the intended computation recoverable, but the terminology is repeated and could mislead implementers because JVPs and VJPs use different autodiff primitives and have different scaling implications.

comments.title: Abstract overstates direct minimization against the true second-order term
comments.quote: Specifically, we parameterize a single Jacobian-vector product (JVP) for each HO step and minimize the distance from the true second-order term.
comments.explanation: The later method section explains that directly evaluating the true second-order term is what the method is designed to avoid. The practical algorithm instead uses approximations, including a Hessian/identity-style approximation, a Lipschitz-continuity lower-bound argument for direction distillation, and sparse or approximate estimation for the scale term. Thus the method is motivated by the true second-order target, but it does not literally minimize distance to that target during the implementable procedure. The abstract wording could make readers think exact second-order targets are available during training.

comments.title: “Constant memory” criterion is ambiguous relative to the stated FMD limitation
comments.quote: ||Constant memory|O|X|O|O|O|**O**|
comments.explanation: The table appears to mark FMD as satisfying “Constant memory,” while the surrounding text says FMD's space complexity grows with the hyperparameter dimension because it maintains forward Jacobian information. The likely intended meaning is constant memory with respect to the unroll horizon, not with respect to model or hyperparameter dimension. Since the table label only says “Constant memory,” a reader could interpret it as contradicting the later high-dimensional-memory limitation. Clarifying the axis with respect to which memory is constant would resolve this.

comments.title: Equating identity inverse-Hessian approximation with one-step lookahead is too strong without qualifications
comments.quote: Bengio (2000) computes the exact inverse Hessian, and Luketina et al. (2016) approximate the inverse Hessian with the identity matrix, which is identical to the onestep lookahead approximation.
comments.explanation: Replacing the inverse Hessian in an IFT-style hypergradient by the identity gives a term like -L_val,w L_train,wλ. A one-step lookahead through w⁺ = w - η∇_w L_train(w,λ) gives a related term, but typically with a learning-rate factor and evaluated through a one-step-updated validation point, often away from convergence. These can be made equivalent only under additional conventions or approximations, such as absorbing η into the hyper-learning rate and ignoring evaluation-point differences. The later narrative treats Luketina et al. as a short-horizon approximation, so the main argument is recoverable, but the exact equivalence claim is overqualified.

comments.title: Pseudocode inputs omit quantities needed to reproduce the Jacobian products
comments.quote:
1: **Input:** The last weight_wT_ and all the previous
weights_w_0_, . . . , wT −_1.
comments.explanation: The update is defined as w_t = Φ(w_{t-1}, λ; D_t), so the Jacobians A_t and B_t depend not only on the stored weights but also on λ, the exact mini-batch sequence, data augmentation randomness, and any optimizer state used at each step. These may be implicit in the surrounding context, but the declared algorithm inputs are not sufficient to reproduce the backward Jacobian products. This matters for RMD/DrMAD-style memory comparisons because an implementation must store, replay, or reconstruct the same update-state information during the backward sweep.

comments.title: Projection step needs an explicit sign or nonnegativity convention for π
comments.quote: Notice that solving Eq. (4) only w.r.t. _π_ is simply a vector projection.
comments.explanation: For a fixed unit vector f_t(w,D), the best scalar approximation to g_t^{SO} depends on whether π is unconstrained or intended to be a nonnegative scale. If π ∈ R, then π* = f^T g and the remaining objective maximizes (f^T g)^2, so an anti-parallel direction is as good as a parallel one. If π is a nonnegative “size,” then the projection should be constrained or clipped, and maximizing positive alignment becomes appropriate. The prose describes π as matching the “size,” but the sign convention is not made explicit, which affects the claimed equivalence of the distillation objective.

comments.title: Direction-distillation update drops expensive norm weights from the derived objective
comments.quote: The following sequential update allows to efficiently evaluate Eq. (12) for each HO step. Denoting _pt_ = ( _γ − γ[t]_ ) _/_ (1 _− γ[t]_ ) _∈_ [0 _,_ 1), we have
comments.explanation: The preceding proxy objective weights each past term by δ_{t,i} = γ^{t−i} ||α_t B_i||_2. Computing these norms for every previous step would itself require the kind of per-step hyperparameter-Jacobian products that the method is trying to avoid. The later recurrence with p_t corresponds to weights proportional only to γ^{t−i}, apparently dropping or approximating the ||α_t B_i|| terms. That may be a deliberate efficiency approximation, but the text says the recurrence efficiently evaluates the derived objective rather than stating that these norm factors are ignored or approximated. This leaves a gap between the lower-bound derivation and the implemented online update.

comments.title: Lipschitz argument over weights and datasets is heuristic as stated
comments.explanation: The intuition is reasonable: nearby weights/data should induce similar normalized hypergradient directions. However, a minibatch or dataset is a discrete object with inputs, labels, augmentations, and ordering, so a Euclidean norm over D and a Lipschitz metric over pairs (w,D) are not naturally defined without specifying an embedding. In addition, a Lipschitz inequality gives only an upper bound on output-space distance; minimizing that bound need not identify the actual minimizer of ||f(w,D)-f(w_i,D_i)|| unless the bound is tight or otherwise monotonic. The later subsampling procedure may be a practical heuristic, but it no longer follows directly from the stated Euclidean-metric lower-bound argument.

comments.title: The endpoint γ = 1 is allowed but later formulas divide by zero
comments.quote: **Algorithm 3** HyperDistill **Algorithm 4** LinearEstimation( _γ, λ, φ_ ) 1: **Input:** _γ ∈_ [0 _,_ 1], initial _λ_ , and initial _φ_ .
comments.explanation: The algorithm input permits γ = 1, but later definitions include denominators such as 1 − γ^t and 1 − γ. Some of these quantities have natural limits as γ approaches 1, but those limiting conventions are not stated. As written, the algorithm admits a value for which core updates and estimators are undefined. The intended restriction should likely be γ ∈ [0,1), or the limiting formulas for γ = 1 should be specified.

comments.title: Scale-estimator derivation and notation overstate the exactness of approximate quantities
comments.explanation: The scale estimator is motivated by a rough proportionality between the lower-bound form and a weighted sum of magnitudes, but the actual projection coefficient depends on alignment between the distilled direction and historical second-order directions, not only on summed magnitudes. In addition, the collected y_s samples are denoted as exact π_s* values even though the procedure later uses a DrMAD-style approximation rather than exact RMD on the true trajectory. The estimator may be a useful heuristic, especially because its parameter is fitted empirically, but the text should distinguish the additional modeling assumption and approximate targets from exact projection coefficients.

comments.title: Dataset subsampling rule leaves fixed batch size ambiguous under rounding
comments.quote: where SS( _D, p_ ) denotes random SubSampling of round ~~o~~ ff( _|D|p_ ) instances from _D_ .
comments.explanation: Using subsampling to approximate dataset interpolation is reasonable, but independently rounding |D|p_t and |D|(1−p_t) need not sum exactly to |D|. Depending on the rounding convention, the distilled batch may contain one more or one fewer example than intended, and recursive subsampling could compound size differences unless controlled. Since the update map typically assumes a fixed minibatch size, the rounding or size-correction rule should be specified for reproducibility.

comments.title: JVP accounting for computational comparisons is inconsistent or underqualified
comments.quote:
FO
O
0
1-step
O

DrMAD
X
199
Neumann IFT
**_△_**
_{_55,60,75_}
...
|**HyperDistill**
O

comments.explanation: The paper states T = 100 inner steps and describes FO, 1-step, and HyperDistill as online methods, but Table 2 reports 50 JVPs for 1-step and approximately 58 for HyperDistill. If these methods update every inner step, 1-step should require about 100 JVPs, and HyperDistill's online pass would also appear to require about one product per step before any amortized LinearEstimation cost. The text also says Neumann IFT “roughly” computes 50 JVPs even though the stated settings cost 55, 60, or 75 by the paper's own formula. Elsewhere, HyperDistill is described as computing “only a single JVP,” which is only accurate for the online update after excluding occasional scale-estimation overhead. These inconsistencies make the computational comparison difficult to reproduce and may overstate the tightness of the efficiency comparison.

comments.title: Label corruption protocol for MetaWeightNet is underspecified
comments.quote: Labels are independently corrupted to random classes with probability 0 _._ 4.
comments.explanation: For MetaWeightNet-style label-noise experiments, the standard setup usually corrupts inner-training labels while keeping validation/query and test labels clean. The paper likely intends this, but the sentence does not specify whether corruption is applied only to the task training split, also to validation/query labels during meta-training, or to meta-test tasks. In an episodic setting, this distinction matters because corrupting validation labels changes the hypergradient target, and corrupting test labels changes the meaning of reported accuracy.

comments.title: Claims attributing convergence improvements to online updating overstate what the comparisons show
comments.quote: HyperDistill shows significantly better convergence than FO and 1-step because it is online and at the same time alleviates the short horizon bias.
comments.explanation: FO and 1-step are also online hyperparameter-optimization methods in the paper's setup, so being online cannot explain HyperDistill's improvement over those baselines. The likely distinguishing factor is the longer-horizon distilled second-order information. Similarly, worse convergence of offline DrMAD relative to FO is suggestive but does not by itself isolate update frequency as the causal factor, since DrMAD also uses a trajectory approximation, a different hypergradient estimate, and potentially different tuning dynamics. The conclusions would be clearer if they separated online-vs-offline comparisons from short-horizon-bias comparisons.

comments.title: Cosine-similarity plots do not directly validate DrMAD’s magnitude estimate
comments.quote: Note that DrMAD approximation (Eq. (16)) is accurate (Figure 3(a) and 3(c)), helping to predict the hypergradient size.
comments.explanation: Figure 3(a) and Figure 3(c), as described, report cosine similarity to exact RMD. Cosine similarity measures direction, not magnitude; a positive scalar rescaling would leave the cosine unchanged. Figure 3(a) for the full hypergradient may be indirectly affected by the relative size of first- and second-order terms, but it is still not a direct validation of scale. The claim that these plots show DrMAD accurately predicts hypergradient size is therefore not fully supported by the cited evidence.

progressive_original__gpt-5.5.label: Progressive Original (gpt-5.5)
progressive_original__gpt-5.5.overall_feedback: The paper addresses an important and practically relevant limitation of gradient-based hyperparameter optimization for meta-learning: obtaining online, memory-efficient hypergradients for high-dimensional hyperparameters without incurring severe short-horizon bias. The motivation is clear, the taxonomy of existing methods is helpful, and the proposed idea of distilling a long-horizon second-order term into a single Jacobian-vector product appears conceptually interesting and potentially impactful. That said, the beginning would benefit from sharper technical precision and clearer positioning: several claims in Table 1 and the surrounding discussion are somewhat coarse-grained, and the distinctions between “online optimization,” “constant memory,” and “horizon > 1” need more formal definitions to avoid ambiguity. The central mechanism—how a second-order term can be reliably distilled, what target is used, and why this does not simply move the computational burden elsewhere—is only sketched at a high level, so the reader is left wanting an early intuitive explanation of the approximation, its assumptions, and possible failure modes. The introduction also occasionally overstates the universality of the proposed criteria and the limitations of prior work; a more nuanced discussion of trade-offs, approximation error, and computational overhead would strengthen the framing. Overall, the paper appears promising and well-motivated, with a potentially strong contribution, but its quality will depend heavily on whether the later sections provide rigorous derivation, convincing evidence that the distillation target is stable and efficient, and fair empirical comparisons against strong unrolled, implicit, and short-horizon baselines.

comments.title: Experimental scope is stated inconsistently
comments.quote: We demonstrate the effectiveness of our method on two different meta-learning methods and three benchmark datasets.
comments.explanation: I initially read this as a high-level summary of the experimental section, so I checked whether the later contribution bullets used the same scope. They do not: the introduction later says, "We empirically demonstrate that our algorithm converges faster and provides better generalization performance at convergence, with three recent meta-learning models and on two benchmark image datasets." These two statements swap both counts: two methods / three datasets versus three models / two datasets. This is not just a stylistic difference between 'methods' and 'models'; the dataset count is also contradictory. The issue is likely easy to fix by aligning the abstract and contribution bullet with the actual experiments, but as written a reader cannot tell which empirical coverage is being claimed.

comments.title: Use of “JVP” appears to describe standard VJPs in reverse-mode differentiation
comments.quote: Secondly, Reverse-Mode Differentiation (RMD) (Maclaurin et al., 2015) back-propagates the Jacobian-vector products (JVPs) from the last to the initial step, which is structurally identical to backprop through time (BPTT) (Werbos, 1990).
comments.explanation: The intended point is clear: RMD propagates sensitivity information backward through the optimization trajectory, analogously to BPTT. However, in standard autodiff terminology, reverse-mode backpropagation computes vector-Jacobian products, not Jacobian-vector products: if an adjoint row/vector α is propagated as αA or αB, that is a VJP, whereas a JVP would have the form Jv and is associated with forward-mode. I checked the later formal algorithms in the provided context, and they indeed use updates like α ← αA_t and g_SO ← g_SO + αB_t, which confirms that the products are VJPs under the usual convention. The mathematical intent is recoverable from the equations, but the terminology is potentially misleading because JVP vs VJP is an important implementation and complexity distinction in hypergradient methods.

comments.title: Abstract overstates direct minimization against the true second-order term
comments.quote: Specifically, we parameterize a single Jacobian-vector product (JVP) for each HO step and minimize the distance from the true second-order term.
comments.explanation: I understand this as an abstract-level description of the distillation objective: the ideal target is the full second-order hypergradient contribution. But the later method section explains that directly solving the objective would require evaluating g_t^{SO}, which is precisely what the method is trying to avoid. The practical algorithm instead uses approximations: a Hessian-identity-style approximation, a Lipschitz-continuity lower bound for direction distillation, and sparse or approximate evaluation for the scale term. Thus, the method is motivated by minimizing distance to the true second-order term, but it does not literally minimize that distance in the implementable procedure. Since this is the abstract, some simplification is acceptable, yet the wording could lead readers to believe that true second-order targets are available or directly optimized during training.

comments.title: “Constant memory” criterion is ambiguous relative to the stated FMD limitation
comments.quote: ||Constant memory|O|X|O|O|O|**O**|
comments.explanation: The table appears to mark FMD as satisfying 'Constant memory', while the surrounding text correctly says that FMD's space complexity grows with the hyperparameter dimension because it maintains forward Jacobian information. I think the authors likely intend 'constant memory' to mean constant with respect to the horizon length, not constant with respect to model or hyperparameter dimension; that interpretation is also consistent with the separate 'High-dim.' row where FMD is marked X. Still, the criterion is introduced in prose as 'memory-efficient' and the table label says only 'Constant memory', so a careful reader could reasonably read the FMD entry as contradicting the later statement that FMD has space complexity linear in hyperparameter dimension. This is fixable by clarifying that the row means constant memory in the number of inner steps / unroll length.

comments.title: RMD products are described as JVPs although the formulas show VJPs
comments.quote: In RMD, we first obtain _αT_ = _[∂][L]_[val] _∂w_[(] _[w] T[T][ ,][λ]_[)] and back-propagate _A_ and _B_ from the last to the first step in the form of JVPs (See Algorithm 1). Whereas RMD is much faster than FMD as we only need to compute one or two JVPs per each step,
comments.explanation: The intended point is clear: RMD avoids materializing full Jacobians and propagates vector-Jacobian-type quantities backward through the unrolled optimization. However, Algorithm 1 uses updates of the form _g_SO ← g_SO + αB_t_ and _α ← αA_t_. With _α_ written as a row adjoint, these are vector-Jacobian products; with the more common column-gradient convention, they would be _B_t^T α_ and _A_t^T α_. They are not Jacobian-vector products in the standard autodiff sense. I checked whether this could be merely a convention issue, but the distinction matters because JVPs are associated with forward-mode products while VJPs are the primitive used by reverse-mode differentiation. Since the paper’s central computational claim is framed around replacing many such products by a single “JVP,” this terminology could mislead readers about the actual autodiff operation and its scaling, even if the underlying algorithmic idea is understandable.

comments.title: Equating identity inverse-Hessian approximation with one-step lookahead is too strong without qualifications
comments.quote: Bengio (2000) computes the exact inverse Hessian, and Luketina et al. (2016) approximate the inverse Hessian with the identity matrix, which is identical to the onestep lookahead approximation.
comments.explanation: I understand the authors’ intent: they are connecting IFT-style hypergradients to the popular one-step approximation, and in many presentations the one-step hypergradient resembles an IFT hypergradient with the inverse Hessian replaced by a scaled identity. However, the statement as written says the two are identical. In the standard bilevel derivation, the IFT term contains something like _- L_{val,w} H_{train}^{-1} L_{train,wλ}_. Replacing _H_{train}^{-1}_ by _I_ gives _- L_{val,w} L_{train,wλ}_. A one-step lookahead through _w^+ = w - η∇_w L_{train}(w,λ)_ instead gives a term scaled by _η_ and evaluated at the one-step-updated validation point, and it is typically used away from convergence. These can be made equivalent only under additional conventions or approximations, e.g. absorbing _η_ into the hyper-learning rate and ignoring evaluation-point differences. The later context does correctly treat Luketina et al. as a short-horizon approximation, so the main narrative is not broken, but the precise equivalence claim is mathematically overqualified.

comments.title: Pseudocode inputs omit quantities needed to reproduce the Jacobian products
comments.quote:
1: **Input:** The last weight_wT_ and all the previous
weights_w_0_, . . . , wT −_1.
comments.explanation: The surrounding text has already defined the update as _w_t = Φ(w_{t-1}, λ; D_t)_, so I initially assumed that _λ_ and the mini-batch sequence _D_1, ..., _D_T_ are implicit global context. That mostly resolves the mathematical intent of Algorithm 1. Still, as written, the algorithm’s declared inputs are not sufficient to compute _A_t_ and _B_t_, because these Jacobians depend on the current hyperparameter and on the exact data batch, augmentation randomness, and optimizer state used at each step. This is especially relevant because the section compares memory and online feasibility of RMD/DrMAD variants: in practice one must either store, deterministically replay, or otherwise reconstruct the same _D_t_ and update-state information during the backward sweep. This is a localized reproducibility/implementation ambiguity rather than a flaw in the derivation.

comments.title: Reverse-mode products are described as JVPs rather than VJPs
comments.quote: In RMD, we first obtain _αT_ = _[∂][L]_[val] _∂w_[(] _[w] T[T][ ,][λ]_[)] and back-propagate _A_ and _B_ from the last to the first step in the form of JVPs (See Algorithm 1). Whereas RMD is much faster than FMD as we only need to compute one or two JVPs per each step
comments.explanation: I understand the authors’ intent: Algorithm 1 propagates products involving the Jacobians A_t and B_t without materializing the full Jacobian matrices, and this is the computational advantage they want to emphasize. However, under the notation used earlier, α is a cotangent/row vector and the operations are α A_t and α B_t. In standard automatic-differentiation terminology these are vector-Jacobian products, or equivalently reverse-mode pullbacks, not Jacobian-vector products. A JVP would have the form A_t v or B_t v with a tangent vector on the right. This distinction matters because JVPs and VJPs have different API calls, different dimensional requirements, and are associated with forward- vs reverse-mode AD. The formulas themselves make the intended computation recoverable, so this is not fatal, but repeatedly calling the reverse-mode operation a JVP could mislead an implementer.

comments.title: Projection step appears to need an explicit sign or nonnegativity convention for π
comments.quote:
Notice that solving Eq. (4) only w.r.t. _π_ is simply a vector projection.

Then, plugging Eq. (5) into _π_ in Eq. (4) and making use of _∥ft_ ( _w, D_ ) _∥_ 2 = 1, we can easily convert the optimization problem Eq. (4) into the following equivalent problem (See Appendix A).

_wt[∗]_[and] _[ D] t[∗]_[match the hypergradient] _[ direction]_[ and] _[ π] t[∗]_[matches the] _[ size]_[.]
comments.explanation: The projection idea is natural: for a fixed unit vector f_t(w,D), the best scalar approximation to g_t^{SO} is obtained by projecting g_t^{SO} onto that direction. But the exact equivalence depends on whether π is allowed to be negative. If π∈R is unconstrained, minimizing ||π f - g||^2 gives π*=f^T g and the remaining objective is to maximize (f^T g)^2, so an anti-parallel f is just as good as a parallel one because π can absorb the sign. If instead π is intended to be a nonnegative scale or “size,” then the projection should be constrained or clipped, and maximizing f^T g rather than its absolute value becomes appropriate. The prose says π matches the “size,” and later derivations seem to rely on positive alignment, but the constraint/sign convention is not made explicit here. This is a localized mathematical ambiguity, but it affects the claimed equivalence of the distillation objective.

comments.title: Proxy direction objective still contains expensive per-step JVP/VJP norms
comments.quote: ˜ ˆ where _δt,i_ = _γ[t][−][i] ∥αtBi∥_ 2 _≥_ 0. Instead of maximizing _πt_ directly, we now maximize _πt_ w.r.t. _w_ and _D_ as a proxy objective.
comments.explanation: The authors correctly identify that the full g_t^{SO} is too expensive and introduce a Hessian/scalar-identity approximation to obtain a more tractable proxy. However, the proxy weights δ_{t,i} still contain ∥α_t B_i∥_2 for every previous step i. Computing these quantities requires evaluating the same kind of hyperparameter-Jacobian product at each past step that the method is trying to avoid; doing this for all i would again cost O(t) products per online step. In the subsequent running-average construction, the weights appear to depend only on γ, not on these ∥α_t B_i∥ terms, so I infer that the authors may be implicitly treating the norms as constant or dropping them. If that is the intent, it should be stated and justified, because otherwise the derivation from the proxy objective to the efficient online update is not faithful to the displayed objective.
